title: Home Dialysis Emergencies
content_markdown:
#### Important Considerations

- Patients who are dialysed at home have undergone extensive training and maintain a record of their treatment.
- Use this and any further information provided by the patient and/or carer – they are very familiar with the process.
- Fistula access is the patient’s life line. Vessels should be preserved for future fistula creation – therefore only **cannulate if necessary** and use the cubital fossa or hand. 
- Most calls to dialysis patients are not related to their dialysis.

#### Removing Patients from the Machine

**If still dialysing, remove the patient from the machine:**

- STOP the dialysis machine pump using the stop pump button: 
- **Do not** turn the machine OFF until the patient is removed.

- **Clamp** the two lines running from the dialysis machine, **and** the two lines from the patient’s fistula / graft / central line.
- Unscrew the luer locks joining the machine lines and the fistula lines.
- Leave the dialysis cannula / lines in situ 
- Reinforce with taping and protect during transit, as these are metal needles and can cause damage to the vessels if mishandled
- Medications and fluid can be administered via either dialysis line.
- If cannula removal is essential, remember there is a **high flow‐in access** (1000ml/minute): 
- Use a protective **mask and goggles**
- Moderate digital pressure is needed on the exit site for 10-15 mins.

#### Dialysis Central Venous Catheter

Identified by yellow connector hubs and the absence of a fistula / graft.

- If still in situ, you may use either line to administer fluid and/or medication using a lure lock syringe connected directly to the yellow cap 
- Never use a needle for access
- **Do not** remove yellow hubs.

- Ensure aseptic technique 
- Clean hub for 15 seconds with an alcohol swab and allow to dry for 30 seconds prior to each new syringe attachment.
- Always aspirate 5ml from the line and discard prior to first use.
- Flush the line after each dose with 10ml of normal saline.

#### Fistula/Graft Cannula

Identified by removable caps and the presence of a fistula / graft.

- If still in situ, you may use either line to administer fluid and/or medication using a luer lock connected to the line after removing the cap 
- Never use a needle for access.

- There is no need to aspirate the line prior to use.
- Flush the line after each dose with 10ml of normal saline.

#### Bleeding

This may occur due to excess thinning of the blood by the heparin used in dialysis. Heparin has a **half life of 92 minutes**. 

- Only light to moderate pressure on cannula site is required **(10-15 mins)**
- Use Dia‐stop / Tipstop (compression dressing) devices where available (digital pressure is the best option).
- **Do not** apply tight bandages.

#### Ruptured Graft / Fistula

- Use sufficient pressure to stop haemorrhage from a ruptured graft / fistula. Two hands may be required. 
- Preservation of the graft / fistula is no longer a consideration.

#### Hypotensive Episode

This can occur due to excess fluid removal. Assess the situation – if the patient / carer can replace fluid (including their blood, from the dialysis machine) this is the best option.

Remember that these patients are often on fluid restrictions. 100-200ml IV fluids will often resolve a hypotensive episode. Consult the patient’s treatment records to ascertain their usual blood pressure. 

**When the paramedic is required to replace fluid:**

- Posture patient with legs elevated.
- Administer oxygen.
- Remove patient from machine (as previous).
- Fluid replacement as per CMG 14 Shock and Hypoperfusion.

#### Haemolysis

Caused by damage to the blood cells due to an inappropriate dialysate (overheating, toxins such as copper, chloramines, bleach, formaldehyde). 
**Signs and symptoms: **
- Chest pain / back pain.
- Dyspnoea.
- Localised burning and pain in access return site.
- Blood turns a characteristic port wine colour.
- Stop dialysis and disconnect patient (as previous).
- Treat symptomatically, as per appropriate CMG (e.g. CMG 6 Cardiac Arrhythmias, CMG 9 Respiratory Distress, CMG 16 Suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome, etc).
- Fluid replacement if indicated, as per CMG 14 Shock and Hypoperfusion.

#### Venous Air Embolism

- Suspect if there is air in the venous (blue) line.
- **Stop dialysis treatment immediately** using the stop pump button. 
- Disconnect patient (as previous).
- Treat with 100% oxygen.
- Posture left‐lateral and flat (i.e. no head elevation).

#### Chest Pain

This may be caused by excess fluid removal during dialysis or other cardiac event.

- Stop dialysis and disconnect patient (as previous) 
- If sufficient time, have patient / carer return their blood from the dialysis machine. 

- Use venous (blue) cannula / line as access for fluid / medication administration, if required. - Treat chest pain as per appropriate CMG (e.g. CMG 16 Suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome, etc).

In the conscious/semi conscious patient, the port line should be primed with the approved designated dose of Lignocaine. This is to be flushed with 1.2-1.5mls of normal saline in order to push the Lignocaine into the medullary space. Wait 2 to 3mins for Lignocaine to take effect.

##### Type
Isotonic crystalloid solution of 0.9% sodium chloride. 
 Contains 151mMol sodium and 151mMol chloride per litre [no schedule] 

##### Presentations
500 or 1000ml of 0.9% sodium chloride solution

##### Actions

- Short acting plasma volume expander

- Also expands interstitial fluid volume

#### Uses

- Initial replacement of fluid, in volume depleted or dehydrated patients. (Volume depletion may be due to loss of blood, plasma or fluid and electrolytes)

- Maintenance of hydration during prolonged patient contact time

- To keep vein open, as IV route for drugs

#### Adverse Effects
Fluid overload 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- **In general, aim to maintain sBP ≥90mmHg: **rate and volume infused is dependent on patient condition. Give 250-500mL boluses, reassess after each bolus.
- **Haemorrhagic hypovolaemia: **aggressive warm fluids. 
- Max. dose: 20ml/kg

- **Traumatic brain injury: **aim for sBP ≥100mmHg 
- No limit on amount - dependent on condition of patient. 
- **TKVO: **10 drops per minute (30ml/h with standard drip set)##### Paediatric
- **Refer to paediatric reference charts for BP goals: **rate and volume infused is dependent on patient condition. Give weight appropriate boluses, reassess after each bolus.
- **Paediatric cardiac arrest: **20ml/kg- **Haemorrhagic hypovolaemia: **aggressive warm fluids 
- Max dose: 20ml/kg
- **TKVO: **10 drops per minute (30ml/h with standard drip set)

##### Type
A naturally occurring gas 

##### Presentations
Compressed gas in all-white cylinders of various sizes, to be fitted with an appropriate pressure-reducing device 

##### Actions
Essential element for aerobic metabolic needs and sustaining life. 
 Oxygen administration improves, or prevents a reduction in, the oxygen content of blood leaving the lungs. 

#### Uses
A wide range of situations, for the treatment or prevention of hypoxaemia

#### Adverse Effects

- Increased risk of fire / explosion – oxygen strongly supports combustion 

- Mucosal dryness and irritation

- Hypoventilation in some COPD patients with CO2 retention (titrate oxygen to maintain SpO2 88 – 92%)

#### Contraindications

- Known paraquat poisoning with SpO2 ≥88%

- History of bleomycin therapy with SpO2 ≥88%

#### Precautions
Neonates (especially pre-term infants) are particularly susceptible to the toxic effects of oxygen. When absolutely required, the lowest effective concentration, for the shortest possible time, should be used to achieve adequate oxygenation.

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric
With the exception of conditions listed below, oxygen should generally be titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥94% (i.e, unless otherwise required, there is no need to push saturations to 100%). 

Deliver oxygen through a device appropriate to the required inspired concentration of oxygen.

- **Nasal cannulae: **1 – 4 litres/min (approx. 24 – 40% oxygen) for low oxygen requirements
- **Oxygen (‘Hudson’) mask: **6 – 15 litres/min (approx. 30 – 65% oxygen) for moderate oxygen requirements
- **Nebuliser: **8 litres/min for moderate oxygen requirements with need for aerosolised medications- **Non-rebreather mask (NRBM): **15 litres/min (approx. 60 – 97% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Bag-valve-mask (BVM): **15 litres/min or demand valve (up to 100% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP): **8 – 15 litres/min (titrated to achieve desired effect/pressure) for high oxygen requirements.
#### Conditions with specific oxygen requirement
- Conditions requiring 100% oxygen delivery (NRBM, BVM) 
- Obstetric emergencies
- Carbon monoxide poisoning
- Unrelieved upper airway obstruction
- Diving emergencies.
- COPD patients with possible CO2 retention – titrate oxygen delivery to maintain SpO2 of 88 – 92%.
- Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) – aim for highest SpO2 possible 
- High flow oxygen (15 litres/min) via nasal cannulae is mandatory during this procedure, in addition to IPPV.

#### Indications
- For patients that require supplemental oxygen.

#### Procedure

Figure 1

##### Nasal Prongs (minimal oxygen requirements – approx 24-40%)

- Attach nasal prong tubing to the oxygen port.

- Select oxygen flow rate. 
- **Note: 15 Lpm continuous during intubation.**

- Place one nasal prong in each nostril and tubing tucked behind each ear.

- Position the slide adjuster underneath the patient’s chin.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 2

##### Hudson Mask (moderate oxygen requirements – approx 30-65%)

- Attach the oxygen tubing to the Hudson mask.

- Connect the oxygen tubing to the oxygen port.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the metal nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 3

##### Non-Rebreather Mask (high oxygen requirements - approx 60-97%)

- Prepare as for Hudson mask.

- Select oxygen flow rate of >10 Lpm.

- Ensure reservoir bag is inflated.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Heparin.text: Remember that these patients are often on fluid restrictions. 100-200ml IV fluids will often resolve a hypotensive episode. Consult the patient’s treatment records to ascertain their usual blood pressure.

##### Type
Unfractioned heparin anticoagulant

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
5000u heparin in 1 ml – glass ampoule

##### Actions

- Inhibits blood clotting

- Combines with antithrombin III, a naturally occurring anti-clotting factor in plasma, and inhibits the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin

- It also interferes with the actions of other clotting factors with fibrin stabilising factor

#### Uses
Patients with an acute ST elevation myocardial infarction who meet the requirements for early percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) procedure

#### Adverse Effects

- Bleeding

- Thrombocytopenia

- Anaphylactoid reactions (rare)

#### Contraindications
Refer to heparin checklist

#### Precautions
Use with care in patients with extensive history of allergies

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting, **contraindicated** during labour

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- 5000u IV – over 30-60 seconds

##### Paediatric
Not used.

#### Special Notes
- This treatment should not delay transport time to hospital.

Patient is symptomatic of STEMI?

YesNo / unsure

- Treat as per normal ACTAS guidelines

Are ALL of the following criteria present:

- Systolic BP Yes to allNo / unsure

- Phone TCH STEMI Doctor and discuss presentation

Are ANY of the following criteria present:

- Known allergy to heparin
- Current use of warfarin / clexane / novel anticoagulants (e.g. apixaban (Eliquis); rivaroxaban (Xarelto); dabigatran (Pradaxa)) 
- Pregnancy or delivery in last 2 weeks- Active pathologic bleeding of any origin- Severe renal or hepatic disease- Severe head injury or facial trauma in the last 3 months- Non-compressible vascular punctures- Clotting problem (e.g. haemophilia)- Known internal bleed within 6 months
Yes / unsureNo to all

- Heparin **not** recommended
- Phone TCH STEMI Doctor

- Heparin recommended
- Phone TCH STEMI Doctor and handover as below: 
- Patient DOB
- Patient age
- Gender- F / M- Signs and symptoms- Time of symptom onset- Diagnosis of ECG- Results of heparin checklist- Recommended / Not recommended- ETA to TCH- If agreed, administer **Heparin 5000u IV**
